quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Galvanic (voltaic) cell"?

options.text: The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction
Strong acids dissociate 100% (Ka very large); weak acids partially dissociate (Ka < 1, typically 10⁻² to 10⁻¹⁰)
Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant)
An electrochemical cell that produces electricity from a spontaneous redox reaction; E°cell > 0, ΔG° < 0

quiz_questions.explanation: "Galvanic (voltaic) cell" is defined as: An electrochemical cell that produces electricity from a spontaneous redox reaction; E°cell > 0, ΔG° < 0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electrolytic cell"?

options.text: A species produced in one elementary step and consumed in a subsequent step; does not appear in the overall equation
An electrochemical cell that uses external electricity to drive a nonspontaneous redox reaction; E°cell  0
The minimum energy required for reactants to reach the transition state and form products; determines the rate's temperature sensitivity
If x/[initial] < 5%, the approximation [HA] ≈ [HA]₀ is valid; avoids solving the quadratic equation for weak acid/base equilibria

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electrolytic cell" is defined as: An electrochemical cell that uses external electricity to drive a nonspontaneous redox reaction; E°cell  0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Salt bridge"?

options.text: A tube filled with inert electrolyte (KNO₃, KCl) connecting two half-cells; allows ion migration to maintain electrical neutrality
The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction
An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility
The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Salt bridge" is defined as: A tube filled with inert electrolyte (KNO₃, KCl) connecting two half-cells; allows ion migration to maintain electrical neutrality. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "ΔG° = -nFE°"?

options.text: A weak acid or base that changes color at a specific pH range; chosen so its color change range includes the equivalence point pH (e.g., phenolphthalein, pH 8.2–10, ideal for weak acid–strong base titrations; bromothymol blue, pH 6.0–7.6, for...
The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry
Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)

quiz_questions.explanation: "ΔG° = -nFE°" is defined as: Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Faraday's law of electrolysis"?

options.text: The point where the indicator changes color; ideally matches the equivalence point; slightly different in practice
The mass of substance deposited/dissolved is proportional to the charge passed: moles = Q/nF, where Q = It (current × time)
An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product
A reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings; ΔH > 0; products are higher in energy than reactants

quiz_questions.explanation: "Faraday's law of electrolysis" is defined as: The mass of substance deposited/dissolved is proportional to the charge passed: moles = Q/nF, where Q = It (current × time). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An electrochemical cell that produces electricity from a spontaneous redox reaction; E°cell > 0, ΔG° < 0"?

options.text: Conjugate pair
Polarizability
Galvanic (voltaic) cell
Secondary battery

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Galvanic (voltaic) cell".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An electrochemical cell that uses external electricity to drive a nonspontaneous redox reaction; E°cell  0"?

options.text: Colligative properties
Selective precipitation
Electrolytic cell
Heterogeneous catalyst

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electrolytic cell".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A tube filled with inert electrolyte (KNO₃, KCl) connecting two half-cells; allows ion migration to maintain electrical neutrality"?

options.text: Activation energy (Ea)
Chromatography
Nernst equation
Salt bridge

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Salt bridge".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)"?

options.text: Heterogeneous catalyst
Common-ion effect
ΔG° = -nFE°
Polarizability

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "ΔG° = -nFE°".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The mass of substance deposited/dissolved is proportional to the charge passed: moles = Q/nF, where Q = It (current × time)"?

options.text: Spontaneous process
Catalyst
Faraday's law of electrolysis
Aufbau principle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Faraday's law of electrolysis".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Electrochemical Cells and Batteries is accurate?

options.text: Electrochemical Cells and Batteries are classified as galvanic (voltaic) cells that convert chemical energy to electrical energy spontaneously (ΔG  0), or electrolytic cells that use electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous reactions (ΔG > 0).
Chemical Equilibrium occurs when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products.
Equilibrium Constants quantify the position of equilibrium.
The common-ion effect reduces solubility when a solution already contains one of the ions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrochemical Cells and Batteries as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries?

options.text: Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).
Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
Galvanic cells have two half-cells connected by a salt bridge (maintains electrical neutrality).
The 5% approximation simplifies calculations when [HA]₀/Ka > 400.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrochemical Cells and Batteries as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Electrochemical Cells and Batteries?

options.text: Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) and strong bases (NaOH, KOH) dissociate completely; weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka or Kb values.
Kc uses molar concentrations, Kp uses partial pressures for gases (Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn).
Each elementary step has a molecularity (unimolecular, bimolecular, rarely termolecular) that directly determines its rate law.
The relationship ΔG° = -nFE° connects thermodynamics to electrochemistry.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrochemical Cells and Batteries as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries?

options.text: Dynamic Equilibrium is reached when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates become equal, with no net change in concentrations.
The choice of method depends on the properties of the mixture components and the purity required.
Alloys (mixtures of metals) modify properties by introducing different-sized atoms.
Faraday's laws relate charge transferred to moles of substance reacted.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrochemical Cells and Batteries as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Electrochemical Cells and Batteries?

options.text: Chemical Equilibrium occurs when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products.

Electrochemical Cells and Batteries are classified as galvanic (voltaic) cells that convert chemical energy to electrical energy spontaneously (ΔG  0), or electrolytic cells that use electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous reactions (ΔG > 0).
This allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly, by combining known reactions.
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrochemical Cells and Batteries as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries?

options.text: Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content
Baking chemistry: using the correct stoichiometric ratio of baking soda (NaHCO₃) to acid (e.g., cream of tartar) to produce the right amount of CO₂ for leavening

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries?

options.text: Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45
Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics

Tempered safety glass as an amorphous solid that, when broken, shatters into small granular pieces rather than sharp shards due to balanced internal stresses from thermal treatment (distinct from laminated glass, which holds together via a...
Water's anomalously high boiling point (100°C vs. H₂S at -60°C) due to extensive hydrogen bonding networks between molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Electrochemical Cells and Batteries applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production
Industrial ammonia production optimizing the ratio of N₂ to H₂ to minimize waste and maximize yield in the Haber process
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield
Swimming pool chemistry: maintaining pH 7.2-7.8 using acid (HCl) or base (NaHCO₃) additions to optimize chlorine disinfection effectiveness

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries?

options.text: Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Lithium-ion batteries exploiting Li's small atomic radius and low ionization energy for high energy density in lightweight portable electronics
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Electrochemical Cells and Batteries in practice?

options.text: Haber process optimization (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃) using high pressure and moderate temperature to shift equilibrium toward ammonia production for fertilizers
Ammonia's pyramidal geometry (sp³, one lone pair) making it a strong Lewis base and critical industrial chemical in fertilizer production (Haber process)
Antacid tablet surface area experiments: crushed tablets dissolve faster than whole tablets because increased surface area increases reaction rate
Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrochemical Cells and Batteries.
